Article Title: Nominee vs legal heir: What is the difference and who has claim on assets? All you need to know
Article Text:
AI image

Who is a Nominee?



Who is a Legal Heir?

Difference between a Nominee and a Legal heir

Aspect Nominee Legal Heir Role Custodian Owner Transfer of Assets Can be done without any legal formalities. Requires testamentary documents for proof of ownership. Powers No power to transact, use, or transfer assets. Power to use and transact assets as an owner. Appointment Owner can appoint anyone, but some institutions specify certain conditions. Determined by succession/personal laws. Legal Right Cannot contest ownership legally. Can contest ownership in a court of law.

When individuals open bank accounts, invest in mutual funds, or buy insurance, they are often asked to name a nominee.However, many people mistakenly assume that the nominee automatically inherits the assets. In reality, the nominee is not always the final recipient — the ownership rights belong to the legal heir, reported ET.Understanding the distinction between these two is essential to ensure that your wealth is passed on to the intended beneficiaries.A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets.The nominee’s role is limited to holding and managing the assets until they are legally transferred to the rightful heirs.According to ET, the nominee cannot sell, trade, or make any transactional decisions about the assets.The account or policy owner can appoint anyone — family, friends, or acquaintances — as a nominee, though financial institutions often prefer close family members.In some cases, the nominee and the legal heir can be the same person, which helps in ensuring a smoother transfer of assets.The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away.To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will or other testamentary proof.If there is no will — a situation known as dying intestate — the heir is determined as per the country’s succession or personal laws, reported ET.While a nominee can initially access the assets, only the legal heir can claim them permanently or challenge ownership in court.Experts quoted by ET emphasise that having clarity on this difference helps avoid disputes and ensures that the savings reach the intended family members or beneficiaries.Therefore, it is advisable to regularly update both nominees and wills to align with one’s estate planning goals.
Article Summary: A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets. The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away. To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will.

Article Title: Life Certificate 2025: Step-by-step guide to generate and submit Jeevan Pramaan online and offline
Article Text:
What is a Life Certificate and why does it matter



Annual submission deadline for pensioners in 2025



1 November and 30 November.

How to generate Jeevan Pramaan online: Step-by-step process



Step 1: Biometric device registration

Step 2: Authentication and certificate generation

Electronic delivery and access of Digital Life Certificate



How can you submit a Life Certificate in 2025



Offline submission at a bank branch or CSC centre

Online submission via Jeevan Pramaan Portal

Submission through the UMANG App

Who needs to submit the Life Certificate



Central and State Government pensioners

Defence pensioners

Family pensioners in the event of the pensioner’s death

Pensioners from public sector undertakings

Key details required before submitting the Life Certificate



Aadhaar Number

Registered Mobile Number linked with Aadhaar

Pension Payment Order (PPO) Number

Bank Account Number where the pension is credited

Name of the Pension Sanctioning Authority

Is Aadhaar mandatory for a digital Life Certificate



Legal validity of Jeevan Pramaan



How Jeevan Pramaan simplifies pensioner verification



Every year, lakhs of pensioners across India must prove that they are still alive to continue receiving their pension. This verification, known as the Life Certificate or Digital Life Certificate (DLC), is a crucial and mandatory step in ensuring that pension payments are made only to eligible recipients, preventing fraud and misuse.Traditionally, this process involved visiting the bank or pension office in person, standing in long queues, and submitting a signed paper certificate. However, with the government’s focus on digitisation and technological advancement, this process has become much simpler, faster, and more convenient. Pensioners can now submit their certificates digitally from the comfort of their homes using Jeevan Pramaan, a secure, user-friendly, and Aadhaar-based platform.A Life Certificate is an official document that confirms a pensioner is alive. Without it, pension payments are automatically paused by the pension disbursing agency. This verification is essential for preventing fraudulent claims and ensuring that pensions reach the rightful beneficiaries.The Digital Life Certificate (DLC), also known as Jeevan Pramaan, is an advanced biometric-enabled version of the traditional paper certificate.Introduced by the Government of India, it uses Aadhaar-based biometric authentication using either fingerprint or iris scan to verify a pensioner’s identity. Once verified, a digital certificate is automatically generated and shared with authorised pension disbursing agencies.This online process eliminates the need for physical verification, providing elderly pensioners with a simpler and more accessible way to maintain their pension benefits.According to PIB, The submission of the Life Certificate is mandatory for all pensioners who wish to continue receiving their pension without interruption. For the year 2025, the standard submission period is betweenTo make the process more comfortable for the elderly, the government allows pensioners aged 80 years and above to begin the submission process earlier, from 1 October 2025. This extended window gives senior citizens more time and flexibility to complete the procedure without facing last-minute difficulties.The Jeevan Pramaan process involves two main steps: registering the biometric device and generating the digital certificate.Before using the service, pensioners or operators must register their biometric device such as a fingerprint or iris scanner. During registration, the user provides their Aadhaar number and mobile number, after which an OTP (One-Time Password) is sent to their mobile. After entering the OTP, they must provide their name and email ID before scanning their fingerprint or iris.Once authentication is complete, the system confirms that device registration was successful, allowing the user to move to the next step.In this step, the pensioner enters personal details such as Aadhaar number, mobile number, name, PPO number, pension type, sanctioning authority, disbursing agency, and bank account details. The system also asks whether the pensioner has remarried or is re-employed.After entering all details, the pensioner scans their fingerprint or iris using the registered biometric device. Upon successful authentication, the Digital Life Certificate is generated, and an SMS acknowledgment with the Jeevan Pramaan Certificate ID is sent to the registered mobile number.This certificate is automatically stored in the Life Certificate Repository, accessible anytime to both pensioners and pension agencies.Once the Digital Life Certificate is created, it can be electronically delivered to the concerned Pension Disbursing Agency (PDA). PDAs can log in to the Jeevan Pramaan portal to view, verify, or download the certificate directly.Pensioners can also download a PDF copy of their certificate by visiting the Jeevan Pramaan website and entering their Pramaan ID or Aadhaar number. This ensures transparency and easy access for future reference.The government provides several options to submit the Life Certificate, allowing pensioners to choose the method that suits them best.Pensioners who prefer traditional methods can visit their bank branch or a Citizen Service Centre (CSC) to submit their Life Certificate physically. Trained staff at these centres assist pensioners in verifying their details and completing the process.For those unable to travel due to age or health issues, India Post Payments Bank (IPPB) offers a doorstep banking service. Pensioners can schedule an appointment, and an IPPB representative will visit their home to complete the submission using biometric authentication. This service is available for both IPPB and non-IPPB account holders.For pensioners comfortable with technology, the Jeevan Pramaan portal provides a completely online process. By visiting https://jeevanpramaan.gov.in, pensioners can submit their Digital Life Certificate using a computer, laptop, or smartphone equipped with a fingerprint or iris scanner.The system uses the Aadhaar platform to authenticate biometric details. Once verification is successful, a Digital Life Certificate is generated and stored in a secure online repository. Pension disbursing agencies can then directly access the certificate, removing the need for physical paperwork.The UMANG (Unified Mobile Application for New-age Governance) app offers another easy option. Pensioners can download the app from the Google Play Store, search for “Jeevan Pramaan,” and select “Generate Life Certificate.”After entering the necessary details such as Aadhaar number, PPO number, and type of pension, pensioners must authenticate themselves using biometric verification. The app then generates a Digital Life Certificate (DLC), which is automatically shared with the pension disbursing authority.All pensioners who receive government, defence, or private sector pensions through authorised disbursing agencies are required to submit their Life Certificate every year. This includes:Failure to submit the certificate within the stipulated period may result in temporary suspension of pension payments until verification is completed.To ensure a smooth submission process, pensioners should keep certain details and documents ready. These include:Having these details ready helps to avoid delays or errors during authentication, whether submitting online or offline.Yes, an Aadhaar number or Virtual ID (VID) is mandatory to generate a Digital Life Certificate. The entire Jeevan Pramaan system is based on Aadhaar authentication, which ensures accuracy and prevents fraud. Pensioners must make sure their Aadhaar is linked with their mobile number and bank account before attempting to generate the certificate.The Digital Life Certificate is fully valid and legally recognised under the Information Technology Act. Pension disbursing authorities across India accept it as an official document, equivalent to the traditional paper-based life certificate.Its biometric verification system makes it secure and tamper-proof, reducing the chances of wrongful pension disbursal or misuse of pension funds.Before the introduction of Jeevan Pramaan, pensioners often faced difficulties in submitting their certificates, especially those with health problems or limited mobility. The digital system has removed these challenges, allowing pensioners to submit their verification anytime and anywhere.By integrating biometric technology and Aadhaar authentication, Jeevan Pramaan has streamlined pension management, improving efficiency for both pensioners and government departments. It also enhances transparency and ensures that genuine beneficiaries receive payments on time.
Article Summary: A Life Certificate is an official document that confirms a pensioner is alive. Without it, pension payments are automatically paused by the pension disbursing agency. This verification is essential for preventing fraudulent claims and ensuring that pensions reach the rightful beneficiaries. The Digital Life Certificate (DLC), also known as Jeevan Pramaan, is an advanced biometric- enabled version of the traditional paper certificate.

Article Title: What are gold, silver hallmarking charges? Check details and importance
Article Text:
What is gold, silver hallmarking?



Hallmarking charges



ALSO READ |

Testing for purity



The importance of hallmarking



Old hallmarking signs



ALSO READ |

Hallmark symbols



HUID in the hallmarked gold jewellery



Key components of hallmark on gold jewellery



Gold, silver hallmarking : Gold and silver jewllery have long been treasured for their beauty and value. However, ensuring their purity and authenticity is important, especially in a market prone to fraudulent practices. This is where the hallmarking scheme comes into play. Here’s what you need to know about hallmarking charges and its procedures:Hallmarking is the process of accurately determining and officially recording the proportionate content of precious metals in articles, such as gold and silver.It serves as a guarantee of their purity or fineness. In India, hallmarking is governed by stringent standards outlined in IS 15820, according to an ET report.Hallmarking charges are applied per article, regardless of its weight. For gold articles, the charge is Rs 45 per article, with a minimum charge of Rs 200 for a consignment. Similarly, for silver articles, the charge is Rs 35 per article, with a minimum charge of Rs 150 for a consignment. It's important to note that additional taxes and levies may apply.A consignment refers to a collection of articles with the same fineness, from which a sample is drawn and assayed for compliance. Different lots may exist within a consignment.Anyone can have their hallmarked jewellery tested for purity at an A&H centre by paying a testing fee of Rs. 200 (for testing using the fire assay method as per IS 1418). These centres must be recognized by the BIS. The list of recognized A&H centres is available on the BIS website under the hallmarking section.The primary objective of hallmarking is to safeguard consumers from counterfeit products. To achieve this, the Indian government made hallmarking mandatory for gold jewellery from June 16, 2021. Since April 1, 2023, jewellers are prohibited from selling any gold jewellery without a 6-digit Hallmark Unique Identification ( HUID ) number.If you bought gold jewellery on or before June 30, 2021, it may have old hallmarking signs. These signs include the purity/fineness mark and the BIS emblem, along with two additional indications: the jeweller's identification number and the assay centre's mark/number.However, starting July 1, 2021, these two indications were replaced with a unique alphanumeric HUID code. The hallmark remains valid for the lifetime of the jewellery, and consumers can sell old jewellery, whether hallmarked or un-hallmarked, to jewellers.The HUID, or hallmark Unique Identification number, is a key component of hallmarking in gold jewellery. It's a unique six-digit alphanumeric code assigned to each hallmarked item, allowing for traceability. Customers can verify the HUID number using the 'Verify HUID' feature in the BIS Care App.Since the introduction of the HUID on July 1, 2021, the hallmark on gold jewellery consists of three marks: the BIS logo, the purity of the article in caratage and fineness, and a unique six-digit alphanumeric HUID number.
Article Summary: Hallmarking is the process of accurately determining and officially recording the proportionate content of precious metals in articles, such as gold and silver. In India, hallmarking is governed by stringent standards outlined in IS 15820. Since April 1, 2023, jewellers are prohibited from selling any gold jewellery without a 6-digit Hallmark Unique Identification number.

Article Title: How to get your CIBIL credit score without a PAN card
Article Text:
Your credit score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity.

What is a CIBIL score?

How to get your CIBIL Credit score without a PAN Card

Factors affecting your CIBIL score

CIBIL credit score : Understanding your creditworthiness is pivotal in today's financial landscape, especially when seeking loans or credit cards. The Credit Information Bureau (India) Limited, commonly known as CIBIL, plays an important role in this regard by providing credit scores to borrowers.According to ET, credit score helps banks make informed decisions about extending credit cards and loans. Your credit history influences your CIBIL score , which is calculated using a special method.Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.1. Go to the official CIBIL website and click on 'Personal CIBIL score,' then 'Get Your Free CIBIL score.' Follow the link provided and fill in all the required details.2. If you don’t have a PAN Card, you can use an alternative identification such as Passport, Voter ID, Driver’s License, or Ration Card. Enter the number of the chosen ID.3. Provide your date of birth, PIN code, and select your state. Finally, enter your mobile number and click on ‘Accept and Continue’.4. Authenticate your identity by entering the OTP (One-Time Password) delivered to your mobile device on the next screen. Click 'Continue' to proceed.5. Choose whether you want to link the device with your account. Select 'Yes' or 'No' based on your preference.6. Upon successful registration, you will see a page confirming that 'You have successfully registered!'7. Click on ‘Go to Dashboard’ to access and view your CIBIL score.The factors influencing the new CIBIL score, as stated on the CIBIL website, include:1. Depth of credit: This refers to the duration of your existing credit history, calculated from the opening date of your oldest credit account.2. Long-term trend of outstanding balances: The overall trend of outstanding balances over time affects your score.3. Transaction history on credit cards: How you manage transactions on your credit cards impacts your score.4. Ratio of actual repayment amount to total amount due: This ratio indicates your repayment behavior and influences your score accordingly.5. Number of new accounts opened and number of accounts closed: Opening or closing accounts can affect your score, so managing these actions wisely is important.
Article Summary: Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.

Article Title: China beats Elon Musk's 'Jesus-level technology' to launch world's first commercial brain chip
Article Text:
The 'Jesus-level technology'



How China's NEO differs from Neuralink



China has moved ahead of Elon Musk 's 'Jesus-level technology' Neuralink in the race to commercialise brain-computer interface technology, approving what is being described as the world's first commercially available brain chip after successful clinical trials.The coin-sized implant, called NEO, has become the first surgically implanted brain-computer interface (BCI) device to clear clinical testing for commercial use, according to the New York Times report.Developed by researchers at Beijing's Tsinghua University and Shanghai-based Neuracle Technology, the device is expected to enter mass production for China's state-run healthcare system.The first version of NEO is aimed at helping patients with spinal cord injuries and paralysis regain control over parts of their nervous system.But researchers, technology companies and investors see brain-computer interfaces as the beginning of a much larger shift in how humans interact with machines.The development comes as Musk's Neuralink continues human trials in the United States but is yet to receive broad regulatory approval for commercial use.Brain-computer interfaces allow signals generated by the brain to be converted into digital commands that can control computers, devices or prosthetics.Musk has repeatedly promoted the technology's potential to transform lives.Speaking via video link at an event in Israel this week, Musk argued that his company, Neuralink, does not receive enough recognition for its achievements."Restoring control of people who are tetraplegics and restoring sight, I think, are pretty big deals," Musk said. "They're sort of what I might call Jesus-level technologies," he added.Neuralink has said its implants could eventually allow users to type, move a computer cursor and operate devices using only their thoughts.Supporters of the technology envision an even more ambitious future, where people could communicate digitally through thoughts or interact with machines without physical input.One reason analysts believe China has moved ahead more quickly is the design of the NEO implant. Unlike Neuralink's N1 device, which requires electrodes to penetrate the brain's cerebral cortex, NEO is designed to sit between the skull and the brain.Eight sensors are placed against the dura mater, the brain's protective outer membrane reducing the invasiveness of the procedure. The implant captures brain signals and sends them to nearby computers, where they are translated into digital commands.According to reports, 36 patients have already tested the device with positive results. Researchers believe the less-invasive approach may help reduce some of the medical risks traditionally associated with brain implants, the Post reported.Experts say brain-computer interfaces could improve the lives of millions of people suffering from neurological disorders, particularly those affecting movement and speech. Potential applications extend beyond paralysis and spinal injuries to conditions such as Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, stroke and depression.However, the technology has also raised concerns about privacy, security and safety. Griffith University cybersecurity expert Dr David Tuffley said the devices offer enormous potential but also create new risks."Brain implants may sound dystopian, but they are a promising part of neuroscience research," he said. At the same time, he warned that the devices could "theoretically allow hackers to access sensitive neural data, such as patients' thoughts and memories.""Hacking may also enable them to impair a patient's cognitive functions such as the ability to concentrate, or even manipulate motor signals to affect how well they move," he said."That's a scary prospect, especially if these devices become more common," he added. Despite rapid progress, researchers also caution that brain implants still face significant biological hurdles.Neuralink's N1 implant is currently being tested in nine patients. One participant, Audrey Crews, recently described the impact the technology has already had on her life."I tried writing my name for the first time in 20 years. I'm working on it," she wrote on X. "It's humbling to know my journey is helping Neuralink refine this technology, which could one day let millions control devices with their minds," she added.
Article Summary: China has approved what is being described as the world's first commercially available brain chip. The coin-sized implant, called NEO, has become the first surgically implanted brain-computer interface (BCI) device to clear clinical testing for commercial use. NEO is expected to enter mass production for China's state-run healthcare system.

Article Text:
A24's Backrooms became its biggest hit ever—now the studio is using that success to fund AI tools with Google.

Backrooms

The first tool is a storyboard generator

A24's fanbase isn't sold

Backrooms

Elden Ring

Fresh off the biggest hit in its history, A24 is taking roughly $75 million from Google and pairing with DeepMind to build AI tools for filmmakers. The Wall Street Journal first reported the figure, which matches what Thrive Capital invested in A24's 2024 round—the one that valued the studio at $3.5 billion. It's the first time Alphabet's Google has taken an equity stake in a film studio, and it lands while, A24's $300 million-plus blockbuster, is still fresh in theatres.The deal is multiyear and nonexclusive, so A24 can still work with other AI firms and DeepMind with other studios. It also keeps Google away from A24's film and TV library. Google cannot train its models on the catalogue, a condition both sides made a point of stating upfront.Work has already started at A24 Labs, the roughly 20-person tech team led by partner Scott Belsky, who came over from Adobe last year. The first project uses AI to generate storyboards—the rough sketches directors use to plan scenes before shooting. Belsky told the Journal the tools "won't look anything like the prompted generation type of AI that people feel uncomfortable with," and argued that most developers pitch AI on the wrong promise: cheaper and faster, when the goal should be better.DeepMind is framing it the same way. CEO Demis Hassabis said the point is to build with artists from the start rather than in isolation, and product VP Eli Collins said breakthroughs come when the technology reaches the best minds in a field. As Wired and Business Insider have noted, that messaging matters—auteur approval is useful currency as Google works to make video models like Veo the ones filmmakers actually want to use.The move puts A24 in line with studios that got there first. Netflix bought Ben Affleck's startup InterPositive in March, Lionsgate is building models with Runway, and Disney's deal with OpenAI fell apart when Sora shut down. But A24 is the one with the most to lose, because its brand runs on emerging directors and a young audience that tends to recoil from AI. Pew found about half of adults under 30 think the technology will harm society, anddirector Kane Parsons—whose film made all that money for the studio—has called generative AI "genuinely harmful."Discontent showed up fast. Within hours, the comment sections under the announcement had filled with fans saying flatly that they were out, some vowing to skip A24 releases altogether. The anger won't show in the numbers right away, but it lands on the reputation A24 has spent a decade building—that of a studio on the artists' side.A24 insists the tools won't make its movies, only assist the people who do. But the line between assisting and making tends to blur once a technology is in the room, and the studio knows it. With Alex Garland's $175 millionadaptation up next, A24 is testing a tricky proposition: whether the audience that prized it for trusting filmmakers over technology will stick around now that the technology is part of the deal.
Article Summary: A24 is taking roughly $75 million from Google and pairing with DeepMind to build AI tools for filmmakers. It's the first time Alphabet's Google has taken an equity stake in a film studio. The first project uses AI to generate storyboards—the rough sketches directors use to plan scenes before shooting.

Article Title: Tech millionaire Bryan Johnson says, ‘I don’t plan on dying, like actually I won’t die; I am 47, but biologically my cardiovascular abilities, lung health, telomeres are age 18; this is why I say make
Article Text:
Bryan Johnson

Bryan Johnson claims his biological age is 18



Bryan Johnson shares 41 things he learned after spending millions on chasing longevity



Tech entrepreneur and anti-aging influencer Bryan Johnson , who is known for his longevity experiments and biohacking lifestyle, has made another bold claim: he says he doesn’t plan on dying — ever. Johnson, 47, told followers on X: “We are the first generation that won’t die, like actually won’t die, I don’t plan on dying. ” He argued that advances in biotechnology and health optimisation could allow humans to radically extend lifespans beyond current limits.Despite being 47 years old, Johnson claims that his body functions like that of an 18-year old. “My cardiovascular abilities are age 18, my lung capacity is age 18, my telomeres are age 18,” he said. He further added that his health regimen has effectively reversed his biological age. For those unaware, Johnson has invested millions into anti-aging research and personal health experiments which includes strict diets, medical monitoring, and regenerative therapies. Johnson stresses on the fact that prioritising health is essential for longevity. “This is why I say make health your top priority, because the difference between being healthy and non-healthy when you’re thinking is like night and day,” he added.The remarks made by Johnson reflect on the growing movement among the tech elites who feel that advancements in AI, biotechnology, and regenerative medicine could make death optional. However, the critics dismiss these claims and treat them as speculative, whereas Johnson insists his personal results prove that radical life extension is achievable.Recently, Johnson distilled years of experimentation and millions of dollars spend on anti-aging into a list of 41 simple health hacks. In a post shared on social media platform X (formerly known as Twitter) Johnson wrote, “This is it. Everything learned spending millions on longevity.” Despite his $2 million-a-year anti-aging protocol known as Project Blueprint, Johnson’s advice is surprisingly accessible. The list shared by Johnson stresses on low-cost, everyday habits: sleep well, exercise regularly, eat whole foods, avoid added sugar and also limit risky behaviour such as smoking or texting while driving.Among his top recommendations:* Sleep is “the world’s most powerful drug.”* Stick to a consistent bedtime before midnight.* Avoid screens an hour before bed.* Walk after meals or do air squats.* Lift heavy things and stretch daily.* Drink water, get morning sunlight, and protect skin at midday.* See friends weekly, avoid plastic, and circulate air indoors.* Limit alcohol, finish coffee before noon, and keep social media use in check.
Article Summary: Bryan Johnson, 47, claims his biological age is 18 and he doesn't plan on dying. Johnson has invested millions into anti-aging research and personal health experiments. His $2 million-a-year anti- aging protocol includes strict diets, medical monitoring, and regenerative therapies.

Article Title: Barracuda unveils new email protection platform for Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace users: All details
Article Text:
“Email is no longer a human-centric communication platform; it’s an operational fabric where humans and AI interact, making it a much bigger target and amplifying the speed, scale and impact of attacks when threats go undetected,”

Barracuda integrated email protection: Key features



Barracuda has introduced a new email security platform designed for Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace users, as organisations face an increasing number of AI-driven phishing and account-compromise attacks. Called Barracuda Integrated Email Protection, the new Integrated Cloud Email Security (ICES) solution uses artificial intelligence to continuously monitor, detect and remediate threats across the entire attack lifecycle. The platform is built on BarracudaONE and is designed for both single-tenant and multi-tenant environments. According to the company, the solution can analyse signals across email, identity, network, data, and application environments to identify threats, explain security decisions, and automatically respond to attacks, even after a malicious message has been delivered.said Rohit Ghai, Chief Executive Officer at Barracuda.The platform includes several AI-powered capabilities to improve email security and incident response. One of the key features is agentic threat investigation and response, which allows AI agents to analyse threat activity, correlate signals across environments and perform automated message clawbacks and remediation actions.Barracuda has also integrated its Bailey AI assistant into the platform. The assistant provides explanations for security decisions and combines verdicts from Microsoft 365, Google Workspace and Barracuda into a single interface.Other features include a unified quarantine system that consolidates Microsoft-quarantined emails into Barracuda's platform and integrated reporting tools designed to show threats blocked before, during and after delivery.The company said the solution deploys via an API-based architecture and does not require changes to mail exchange records or major modifications to existing email workflows.The launch was accompanied by research from Barracuda showing how quickly modern phishing attacks can spiral. A simulated, multi-step, AI-driven attack found that one phishing email led to identity theft, multi-factor authentication (MFA) bypass, and endpoint compromise within minutes, the company’s Red Team simulation found.The research also found that one in seven compromised accounts is used to launch additional attacks, helping threat actors move laterally across systems and identities. Barracuda said these findings underscore the need for continuous monitoring and automated response capabilities as AI-assisted cyberattacks become more sophisticated.Commenting on the Indian cybersecurity landscape, Parag Khurana, Country Manager, India at Barracuda Networks, said: “AI-driven cyberattacks in India are moving faster than many security stacks can respond. Threat actors can now move from a personalised phishing lure to full account and endpoint compromise within minutes, often bypassing the legacy controls many organisations still rely on.Barracuda said the platform is powered by its Barracuda IQ engine and analyses approximately 1.5 billion URLs every day as part of its threat detection process.
Article Summary: Barracuda has introduced a new email security platform designed for Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace users. Called Barracuda Integrated Email Protection, the new Integrated Cloud Email Security (ICES) solution uses artificial intelligence to continuously monitor, detect and remediate threats across the entire attack lifecycle.

Article Title: Apple CEO Tim Cook confirms iPhone price hike is coming: Here's how much your next iPhone could cost
Article Text:
Tim Cook confirms Apple price hikes are "unavoidable"—your next iPhone could get costlier as soon as this month.

Why the iPhone could get a price hike within next few weeks

What you'd pay now, and what's coming in the fall

Apple's current iPhones could get more expensive within weeks, not months. After CEO Tim Cook warned that price increases are "unavoidable," analysts and supply-chain watchers now expect Apple to raise prices on its existing iPhone 17 lineup as soon as this month—well before the iPhone 18 arrives in the fall. "We've been trying to shield our customers from the increases, but the situation has become unsustainable," Cook told The Wall Street Journal last week, in an unusually blunt admission from a company that rarely signals its pricing in advance.The cause is a global memory-chip shortage Apple can't absorb any longer. AI and data-center companies are buying up DRAM and NAND flash storage faster than suppliers like Samsung, SK Hynix and Micron can make it, and prices for both have quadrupled since last year. Cook called the swing unlike anything in his four decades in the business: "This is a hundred-year flood." Samsung, Microsoft, Sony and Dell have already raised their own prices, and Apple—which has guarded its margins fiercely for years—is expected to follow.Cook declined to say which devices would rise or when, but Bloomberg's Mark Gurman believes the timing of his comments points to increases that are "imminent"—and explicitly "not a fall thing" tied to the iPhone 18 launch. Gurman links the move to Apple's annual Back to School sale, the June promotion offering students and teachers free accessories or gift cards with a Mac or iPad. It typically lands around WWDC—in three of the last five years, 8 to 10 days after the keynote—which could put it as early as this week, possibly bundled with the price rise to soften the blow.Supply-chain leaker Ice Universe echoed the timing over the weekend.Apple's lineup currently starts at $599 for the iPhone 17e, $799 for the iPhone 17, $999 for the iPhone Air, $1,099 for the iPhone 17 Pro and $1,199 for the iPhone 17 Pro Max—numbers that may not hold much longer. There's no official word yet on what this year's models will cost, but any increase is expected to stay under $100.The fall iPhone 18 Pro looks set to climb further still: to protect its margins, Apple would need to add about $270 to the next Pro, research firm TechInsights estimates, pushing it to roughly $1,299, while the WSJ's own analysis puts the ceiling at $1,399 once a pricier camera system is factored in. Macs and iPads could move first—Apple quietly raised the Mac Mini's starting price last month. The takeaway from the analysts is blunt: if you were planning to buy a current model, sooner may beat later.
Article Summary: Apple's current iPhones could get more expensive within weeks, not months. CEO Tim Cook warned that price increases are "unavoidable" Analysts and supply-chain watchers now expect Apple to raise prices as soon as this month. The cause is a global memory-chip shortage.

Article Title: How IBM’s AI tool 'Bob' completed 10 years of Wimbledon development work in 9 months
Article Text:
“We’ve used AI to help us deliver what is effectively 10 years’ worth of development in the past nine months in time for this year’s championship. That wouldn’t have been possible with the team of 50 people, but it’s possible with a team of 50 people and a team of digital workers, which is what we refer to as IBM Bob,”

How IBM Bob accelerated Wimbledon development



"Court 19"

“Court 19, the hidden court that nobody realises [is] where all of this incredible technology is being brought to life.”

“IBM Bob helps them to do things in a matter of minutes that would take weeks,”

“We use it to create a knowledge graph of all these interactions online,” said Stanhouse. “And then we’re able to transfer all of those to the new platforms in 47 minutes. So, something that would take four or five specialists about two to three months to execute took [IBM Bob] about a week to develop and then 47 minutes to transfer the assets,”

“The way to do that is through both traditional broadcasts and, more importantly now, via digital channels,”

“We got 18 billion impressions last year, many of which were through the digital channels and through the innovations that we’ve designed across our multiple platforms alongside partners, like IBM,”

“A lot of people interact with our technology every day and have no idea about it. But through what we do with the AELTC, we put IBM technology in people’s hands and they’re able to touch and feel and be inspired about what’s possible when they work with us,”
Article Summary: IBM Bob helps Wimbledon deliver 10 years' worth of development in nine months. Stanhouse: “Court 19, the hidden court that nobody realises [is] where all of this incredible technology is being brought to life.”

Article Title: In 1990, Alan Emtage was building a tool to find FTP files, unexpectedly found the internet search we still use today
Article Text:
Alan Emtage, creator of Archie, the pioneering search tool that helped lay the foundations for modern internet search. Image Credits: Wikipedia

The student behind Archie

Discovery of a search tool before the web existed

The Archie search interface was one of the earliest tools designed to help users locate files across the growing internet. Image Credits: Wikipedia

The unexpected legacy of a file finder

Long before billions of people typed questions into search engines every day, finding information online was often a slow and frustrating process. Users had to search individual file archives one by one, hoping the material they needed was stored there. As the Internet grew, this manual approach became increasingly impractical.In 1990, a student at McGill University named Alan Emtage was trying to solve that problem. Working with the university's network systems, he created a program called Archie that automatically collected file listings from FTP servers and organised them into a searchable database. What began as a practical solution for locating files unexpectedly became one of the earliest foundations of internet search.Born in Barbados, Alan Emtage developed an interest in computers at an early age. He later moved to Canada and studied at McGill University in Montreal, where he worked in the university's School of Computer Science.During the late 1980s, universities made up a significant part of the growing Internet. Researchers, students, and institutions regularly exchanged software and documentsthrough File Transfer Protocol (FTP) servers. These servers functioned as digital storage locations where users could download files from remote computers.As more FTP archives appeared, locating a specific file became increasingly difficult. Users often had to know which server contained the information they wanted before they could access it. Emtage encountered this challenge regularly and began wondering whether the process could be automated.Today, search engines are associated with websites and web pages. However, when Archie of Texas's World Changers profile , techniques pioneered by Alan still show up in modern search engines like Google The basic idea introduced by Archie endured over time. Later search engines applied the same approach on a much larger scale and to different kinds of content. Even so, the underlying process remained recognisable: gather information from multiple sources, organise it into an index, and allow users to search that index rather than the original sites themselves.That is why it is seen as a milestone in internet history, not just an early experiment. It made online information easier to navigate and pointed the way toward modern search engines.When Alan Emtage created Archie, he was not trying to build a global technology company or transform how humanity accessed information. He was attempting to solve a practical problem faced by users of a growing computer network. Yet the solution proved larger than the problem that inspired it.As the Internet expanded during the 1990s, search became one of its most essential functions. Later search engines introduced web crawling, page ranking, and sophisticated algorithms, but they continued to rely on a principle Archie had already demonstrated.Looking back, Archie was much more than a file locator program. It was one of the earliest responses to a major problem created by the Internet.
Article Summary: Archie was one of the earliest tools designed to help users locate files across the growing internet. It was created by Alan Emtage, a student at McGill University in Montreal, Canada, in 1990. The basic idea introduced by Archie endured over time. Later search engines applied the same approach on a much larger scale.

Article Title: In 1982, Scott Fahlman, typing a joke marker on a message board, unexpectedly found the emoticon we still use today
Article Text:
Scott Fahlman, creator of the first digital emoticon. Image Credits: Wikimedia Commons

The computer scientist behind the digital smile

First digital Emoticon, created from a simple keyboard symbol that transformed online communication. Image Credits: Wikipedia

From campus message boards to the wider internet

Long before smartphones filled conversations with colourful emojis, digital communication faced an ordinary problem where people could not always tell when someone was joking.In September 1982, computer scientist Scott Fahlman was taking part in discussions on an electronic bulletin board at Carnegie Mellon University. The online conversations were lively, but they often led to confusion because written messages lacked facial expressions, tone of voice, and body language. A sarcastic remark could easily be mistaken for a serious statement. Looking for a way to differentiate humour from serious comments, Fahlman proposed a simple solution: a sideways smiley face made from keyboard characters.Born on March 21, 1948, Scott Elliott Fahlman developed an early interest in science and computing. He later joined Carnegie Mellon University, where he became a professor in the School of Computer Science and conducted research in artificial intelligence, programming languages, and machine learning.Universities became one of the few institutions by the early 1980s where a lot of people used computers to communicate via the network.People, including students, teachers, and researchers, exchanged their views instantly thanks to the message boards. Unfortunately, such early forms of communication were missing a number of social signals that humans used to send each other during face-to-face conversations.With the increase in the frequency of discussions, misunderstandings were common. Jokes were often understood literally, which caused confusion and, in some cases, even created panic. Fahlman started thinking about whether there was a way to use a simple signal to differentiate jokes from other comments.The recovered bulletin board thread from Carnegie Mellon illustrates the case with great clarity. On 19 September 1982 at 11:44, Fahlman suggested using ":-)" for joking messages and ":-(" for serious messages. This page is valuable since it gives an opportunity to see the original wording, exact date, and the context in which the emoticon was created. In addition, the page indicates that the thread was retrieved later on the backup tape, which increases its credibility. In a realm in which many stories about the origins of things become blurred with time, this is particularly clear. Emoticons were not invented as an ornament; they were invented as a tool.The idea's simplicity was precisely what made it so effective. Fahlman was not trying to invent an entirely new language or change Internet communication. The problem that he was trying to solve was limited to one discussion forum. However, the idea proved adaptable enough to travel across different computer systems and cultures.In his account " Smiley Lore," Scott E. Fahlman explains that the idea quickly spread beyond Carnegie Mellon. Since the symbols only needed a normal keyboard to type, they could be used anywhere. As universities’ computers were networked with each other and with other research organisations, users started using Fahlman’s faces in their own conversations. Over time, people came up with new combinations to indicate surprise, amusement, scepticism, and many other moods. What started as a means of identifying jokes soon developed into a full visual lexicon for online communication.By the 1990s, emoticons had become an integral part of email and chat room correspondence. The popularity of emoticons showed the increasing necessity to convey emotions through text only. Decades later, graphical emojis were built on the same system. However, the original ":-)" symbol remained as a reminder of discovery.
Article Summary: Computer scientist Scott Fahlman created the first digital emoticon in 1982. He wanted a way to differentiate between jokes and serious comments. Since the symbols only needed a normal keyboard to type, they could be used anywhere. By the 1990s, emoticons had become an integral part of email and chat room correspondence.

Article Title: In 1994, Jaap Haartsen set out to replace short cables and accidentally created Bluetooth
Article Text:
Bluetooth logo symbolizing seamless wireless connection and smart technology. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A practical solution to a common problem

Why the wireless design succeeded

Dr. Jaap Haartsen, inventor of Bluetooth. Image Credit: National Inventors Hall of Fame

From a 1994 invention to a global standard

The hidden link to something invisible

In 1994, Dutch engineer Jaap Haartsen was not trying to create a global brand. All he wanted to do was find a way to get rid of the short cables that connected nearby electronic devices. What seemed like a simple task became Bluetooth, the wireless technology now built into phones, laptops, headsets and keyboards.In other words, there were no grand plans at the outset. It was a simple solution to a rather mundane problem, namely, too many cables and a lack of mobility.The main point of the invention is also rather clear. Engineers needed a wireless connection between nearby devices on a table, in a bag or on a person's body. They did not want to build a global communications network. They just wanted to get rid of short but annoying cables.Such a limited scope played an important role in shaping the technical standard from the start. According to a peer-reviewed historical review indexed in PubMed , early Bluetooth version 1.0 was developed as a cable replacement technology specifically intended for such items as headsets and telephones. Thus, looking at the narrow technical context, we can say the invention emerged from a specific technological need rather than a general desire for wireless technology.Such a cable replacement concept is also the key factor in the success of the Bluetooth technology. Indeed, it suited consumer electronics well because it relied on short-range connections, ease of use, and quick pairing.Bluetooth succeeded because it matched existing user behaviour: people wanted devices to connect without complicated setup. What originally emerged as an efficient solution to a desktop problem turned into a general-purpose linking technology for consumer products.This helped Bluetooth stay relevant as other connection technologies fell out of use. Due to its short-range operation and low consumption, Bluetooth technology was an excellent choice for personal gadgets because energy savings and user-friendly features were crucial here, rather than the possibility of connecting over great distances. The technical team behind the technology focused on ease of use, which helped its durability. The invention did not solve a temporary problem; it provided a flexible solution for future changes in the field.One should clearly separate the initial concept from the later official standard. The work done by Haartsen in 1994 belonged to the period of invention of the technology rather than its commercial release. Like other significant technologies, it took several years for Bluetooth to move from conception to mass use.The timeline offers a different perspective on how the technology developed. The invention was not an instantaneous consumer device but a technological solution that would require time to develop into a standard that others can utilise. The collaborative process helped it spread across multiple brands.An extensive historical study provided by the Sensors website proves this approach, demonstrating how the technology evolved from the 1990s cable replacement into a more general connectivity technology platform. This long evolution process from the laboratory idea to worldwide implementation is one of the key aspects of the success story.Today, Bluetooth technology has one obvious characteristic: how normal and ubiquitous it has become. The idea began as a way to replace short copper wires and is now built into billions of devices worldwide. The technology aligned closely with both engineering needs and consumer demand.The lesson to be learned from this example is that the most practical innovations tend to originate quite simply. The original concept left room for further improvement. Its function is simple: it connects nearby devices without a physical connection. It is a rare case where the original problem closely matches the product that emerged. Technology has changed significantly, but the basic human need remains the same.
Article Summary: Bluetooth was invented in 1994 by Dutch engineer Jaap Haartsen. The technology is now built into phones, laptops, headsets and keyboards. It was developed as a way to replace short copper wires. It took several years for Bluetooth to move from conception to mass use.

Article Title: In 1991, Linus Torvalds shared a small operating system project online and unexpectedly created Linux
Article Text:
Tux is a penguin character and the official mascot of the Linux kernel. Originally created as an entry to a Linux logo competition. Image Credit: Wikipedia

Why scientists depend on Linux

Linus Benedict Torvalds (born 28 December 1969) is a Finnish and American software engineer who is the creator and lead developer of the Linux kernel since 1991. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

From a hobby to a global infrastructure

A platform that is used consistently

The history of Linux is among the most unusual stories of recent technological progress. In 1991, Finnish undergraduate Linus Torvalds uploaded his experimental operating system to the web. Back then, this was just a minor personal project. The creator was not promoting it as something fully formed for everybody’s admiration, and no one would have thought that this piece of software could eventually become a full-fledged computing environment.These days, Linux runs on many devices around us. The program has evolved from a student project into critical infrastructure used around the world.Linux has found a solid footing in scientific environments. Unlike many early programs, it survived because it remained useful to people doing demanding work. In the fields of data processing and programming, everything depends on stability.Scientific papers often use Linux as an infrastructure component. For example, one paper referenced in PubMed uses a high-performance Linux cluster in a scalable workflow. As stated in the paper, scientists who rely on a certain platform to process large amounts of data make a sensible decision.The system should be manageable and reliable under stress.In addition, Linux plays a crucial role in the computational biology field. In general, software ecosystems usually crystallise around several reliable platforms, and Linux becomes one of them as the default system to conduct crucial research.Nowadays, Linux is no longer confined to the scientific laboratories of universities. Its scope of applications has expanded to include public health software, stacks, and other programs that can influence millions of people's lives.For example, the World Health Organization says on its website that AccessMod works on multiple platforms, including Linux via virtualisation. Thus, cross-platform compatibility of software demonstrates that Linux remains a popular and reliable platform to run crucial applications.Its durability lies in the open-source approach. Users can inspect the code, make changes, and distribute it. The community does not have to wait for a closed company to release updates; it can test what works best and fix problems directly.Unlike some software, Linux did not need to become a commercial hit to matter. It was needed because demand for adaptable systems was growing. The key to any platform’s survival is usability, and Linux proved stable over time.According to an article published in PLOS Computational Biology , Linux support is one of the main elements of usability in the complex software environment.Torvald’s 1991 post was just a starting point with an unintended outcome. It suggests that important technological histories can begin with simple acts of sharing. Linux survived not because it stayed static, but because it evolved with users’ needs, which is why it remains an important tool today.
Article Summary: In 1991, Finnish undergraduate Linus Torvalds uploaded his experimental operating system to the web. Back then, this was just a minor personal project. Today, Linux runs on many devices around us. The program has evolved from a student project into critical infrastructure used around the world.

Article Title: In 1973, Bob Metcalfe sketched a way to link office machines and accidentally created Ethernet
Article Text:
Robert "Bob" Melancton Metcalfe (born April 7, 1946) is an American engineer and entrepreneur who contributed to the development of the internet in the 1970s. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

A simple memo with a huge future

The Xerox Alto is a computer system developed at Xerox PARC in the 1970s. Image Credit: Wikipedia

From a quick note to proper architecture

Surviving the age of floppy disks

However, in May 1973, Bob Metcalfe, an engineer working in the renowned research lab at Xerox PARC, experienced a serious problem. The laboratory was full of innovative personal computers and high-tech printers, but these ingenious machines were unable to communicate with each other. A solution to a local problem connecting equipment inside one building led to a technology now used for data transmission in homes, offices, and data centres worldwide.Metcalfe tried to solve the problem of tangled wires and devised a "broadcast communication network" that let machines share one local line without chaos.According to a retrospective article from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology , the whole idea about Ethernet was formulated in a simple office memo written by Metcalfe on May 22, 1973. This document places the birth of computer networking in one room at one specific moment in time.Rather than having an individual costly connection for each computer to a printer, he thought about a network where they share a connection to one device. What made this early concept notable was its simplicity. It was designed as a building-wide network, which had local communications in mind, but was technically perfect enough to be scalable in the future.In order to make the idea work, a set of rules had to be established. Obviously, many computers talking at once would create electrical noise on a wire. To prevent this, Metcalfe and his team developed a technical approach that divided information into packets and managed their delivery to reduce collisions.According to the report by the University of Texas at Austin , Metcalfe received a Turing Award, the highest recognition in computer science, for his contributions to developing and popularising Ethernet technology. That transformation of a quick office note into a technical specification helped establish the system's reliability and discipline.Many developments from the 1970s have since been outshone or replaced by quicker alternatives. One reason Ethernet has survived is its ability to solve the enduring problem of how machines share information efficiently. Whereas most people nowadays use wireless internet via Wi-Fi, it is wired Ethernet that continues to be the unseen backbone of the modern internet.Yet, the principle has remained unchanged since.
Article Summary: Robert "Bob" Melancton Metcalfe (born April 7, 1946) is an American engineer and entrepreneur who contributed to the development of the internet in the 1970s. In May 1973, an engineer working in the renowned research lab at Xerox PARC, experienced a serious problem. He devised a "broadcast communication network" that let machines share one local line without chaos.

Article Title: Tesla Bull Ross Gerber says Elon Musk’s Starlink can make ‘real AI phone’ to challenge iPhone, users ‘correct’ him saying Google Pixel can already ‘do all’ that ...

Google Pixel users push back

Google’s AI-first pixel strategy

Apple’s market dominance

Poll How important is satellite connectivity for the smartphone you want? Very important Somewhat important Not important at all

Musk’s Starlink phone hints

Tesla bull and CEO of Gerber Kawasaki, Ross Gerber has suggested that Elon Musk’s Starlink could one day launch a device which will be capable of challenging Apple’s iPhone dominance, according to a repot by Benzinga. But users quickly countered him saying Google Pixel smartphones already deliver much of that functionality. In post on social media platform X (formerly known as Twitter), Gerber wrote that Starlink has an opportunity to create “truly great AI phone” built around satellite connectivity, exceptional battery life, and an AI-first operating system. “Tell the phone what you want it to do and it does it for you,” he said, before asking: “Who will make a real AI phone first?”When one pointed out that Google Pixel smartphones already offer Gemini-powered voice commands and satellite communication features, Gerber dismissed the claim. “You can tell if you want a pizza from Prince Street at 6 p.m. and it happens? No,” he replied.Pixel owners argued that Gemini can already handle most of the workflow Gerber described — opening food delivery apps, locating restaurants, adding items to a cart, and navigating the ordering process. The only step requiring user input is payment authorization, a safeguard against unintended purchases.Google has heavily promoted AI across its latest Pixel lineup. The Pixel 10, powered by the Tensor G5 chip, runs advanced Gemini AI experiences directly on-device. Features include:The debate comes as Apple faces scrutiny over its AI strategy following mixed reactions to recent Siri AI announcements.Despite this, Apple remains the dominant smartphone vendor in the U.S., holding 61.51% market share in May 2026, compared with Samsung’s 20.74% and Google’s 3.86%.Apple reported $56.99 billion in iPhone revenue for its fiscal second quarter, up from $46.84 billion a year earlier, underscoring its continued strength in the market.Musk himself hinted earlier this year that a Starlink smartphone could eventually emerge, potentially leveraging satellite connectivity and AI integration. While speculative, such a device could align with Musk’s broader vision of AI-driven consumer hardware.
Article Summary: Ross Gerber, CEO of Gerber Kawasaki, suggested that Elon Musk’s Starlink could one day launch a device. Gerber wrote that Starlink has an opportunity to create ‘truly great AI phone’ built around satellite connectivity, exceptional battery life, and an AI-first operating system. Users quickly countered him saying Google Pixel smartphones already deliver much of that functionality.
